sentences.text: My name is Bruno.
B-R-U-N-O.
Zwass, Z like in zebra, W-A, and double S like in Sam.
I was born in Breslau, in Germany, on April the 27th, 1923.
I remember them very well.
We had close family contact with our maternal grandparents.
There were no paternal grandparents.
They had deceased long before.
But my maternal grandparents, from Gorlice, with them, we had close and constant contact.
Both.
But mostly we went to visit them.
My grandmother was a simple woman from a rich home.
Her parents had a large business of fur and beef.
And she was married to my grandfather when she was only 16.
She, herself, was rather primitive, but very religious-- and that was her primitivity.
[LAUGHS] But my grandfather, he was a man that was bought into my grandmother's home for cash because he was learned.
He was only 18 when he married, but he was known to be wise and well-learned.
And he-- he later in life became an insurance agent for an Italian insurance company.
That was rare, because most Jews in eastern Poland at that time didn't even know how to write in any language, including Hebrew and all.
But because of his learned status, and because of his wisdom-- and he had a-- a cynical vein that was humorous-- he became the judge of the city, known as a diyan.
Diyan is a judge.
When two Jews had a conflict, it was my grandfather who usually settled it.
His word was law amongst the Jews of Gorlice.
And he was in very high respect.
There is a book that was written, after the war, in Israel, in which he's presented very favorably.
Well, that was a time when a rich Jew suited his daughter with somebody that brought [YIDDISH], or--
Respect to the family for what amounts to-- to a-- to a business transaction.
My grandfather not only didn't fall in love or so, he never knew her.
After the wedding, he was looking around who his wife is, but-- but he found her only because he started calling her name.
He knew her name.
But he never saw his wife before the wedding.
No, this I don't know.
I wasn't there.
[LAUGHS]
But that is the way it went in those days.
Still, the marriage lasted all through the years, produced five wonderful children, and this leads to a very touching story, by the way.
Because my grandfather was a heavy smoker.
And it wasn't unusual that my grandmother would yell over him because he was storing the ashes, or coughing a lot with expectoration and so, like old smokers do.
But when the children asked grandma, why do you tolerate that?
She would consistently say, this is not your business.
You must be respectful to your grandfather.
And I don't want to live one day without him.
But when we asked grandfather why he tolerates the yelling of my grandmother and so, we got the same answer.
You respect your grandmother.
And I don't want to live one day without them.
In the end, the Germans accommodated them.
We know from eyewitness accounts that my grandfather-- who was frail and debilitated-- was rushed through the streets to the place the Germans had prepared for embarkation to death camps.
But he couldn't keep pace, so he was beaten on the street until he fell.
That's when my grandmother turned around, trying to aid him.
The Germans, then-- actually, their-- their helpers, according to eyewitness accounts, it was Ukrainians-- started beating up on her.
They clubbed them to death in the street, both of them.
And they succumbed.
They died in an embrace.
None of them ever lived a day without the other-- courtesy of the Germans.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] You were led to this story unwillingly.
But it is a story I cannot forget.
Yes.
Well, let me first tell you-- maybe I better start with Germany where I was born.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We-- my father was a small merchant of clothes-- workmen's clothes.
And we had a small store on [NON-ENGLISH] '39, in Breslau.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I was born there and went to the regional public school there.
Few Jews, and the rest of the children were all German children.
We were friends with the German children that I went to school with.
My closest friend was a German.
His name was Manfred Buchhalt-- Manfred Buchhalt.
[PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS] We had a generous mutual social life.
We went to school together.
We played together.
We came to one another's home.
And our parents were friendly on both sides.
Until his older brother-- his name was Wolfgang-- joined-- he was at the time already in college-- and he joined the Hitler Youth-- the Hitler Youth.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This was the first time I saw Wolfgang in a brown uniform with a swastika and so.
And soon thereafter-- I remember it well.
Even though Wolfgang, in the beginning, was making me comfortable-- I'm now talking about 1932, before the takeover of Germany.
He thought-- he himself thought, honestly-- that it doesn't have anything to do with me.
He was too young and too foolish to understand what is developing.
But soon thereafter, I was told that we can no longer be in one another's home, or be seen together, because these are the new directives that rule the party.
And soon there was a takeover of Germany by the Nazi party by bringing Hitler to power.
From then on it went downhill.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And things got to be more and more dangerous.
But in 1932, when I was a child, I remember well the weekends when the communists, mainly the communists, were fighting the Nazis in the streets of Breslau.
This was a frequent occurrence, and amongst the communists at that time, there was a significant constituents of Jews.
Because this was the only truly organized opposition to Hitler at that time, 1932.
All of it vanished after 1933 because communist activists and opposition leaders and members were eliminated, whether they were Jewish, German, or any other way.
My parents were progressive people, but traditionally Jewish.
We were not exceedingly religious, but we carried on with Jewish tradition in the sense of Sabbath celebration, a Seder, or holidays.
And there was a sort of kosher in our-- in our house, but not-- it was not enough to make my grandfather comfortable eating in our house.
Older.
Not much older, just 18 months.
Very well.
He was a gifted one.
He was a straight-A student, and generally liked and-- and considered promising.
He was, yes.
Not at all.
No.
No.
I was the black sheep, actually.
I was--
I-- I had the wildest ideas as a child.
[LAUGHS] And I was punished a lot.

Oh, if we were not prepared for the-- for the latest Latin lecture, I took the whole-- the whole school, the whole class, to-- to-- to the river where we-- [LAUGHS] where we-- where we engaged in games with kayak riding and so.
It-- I was considered disruptive.
Yes.
Whole class.
I was a leader, yes.
[LAUGHS] Yeah.
I was punished for this.
We had corporal punishment both at home and in school.
Oh, there were beatings.
No.
With a hand.
There wasn't any killing, no.
In school, yes.
In school, they had sticks.
In those days, corporal punishment in school was routine.
And I was a disruptive student.
But this changed later as I matured, of course.
This wasn't so in medical school.
Hmm?
Well, I had a professor who came to me and he said, this is not a cheder.
[LAUGHS] Here, you must learn to follow instructions, accept criticism and supervision, because without that, you will flunk very fast.
I took this to heart, and I never forgot it.
I made it well, yes.
I made it rather well.
In medical school, I really had no problems.
I felt no anti-Semitism until, actually, 1932, in Germany.
In Germany, anti-Semitism became noticeable, at least to me, in '32, as I started this story with the two brothers, Manfred and Wolfgang.
And we saw the clashes between the communists and the Nazis on weekends in the streets.
But soon thereafter, there was already before the Nazi takeover, there were outright assaults on Jewish businesses, homes, painting the windows with urine, and assaulting Jewish people in the street.
And we could tell that something is developing of a serious nature.
I was too young to be able to judge the situation, but my parents soon knew that it is time to get away from Germany.

And so in 1934-- that is, to put it in perspective, a year and a half after the Nazi takeover, which occurred in January of 1933-- we left [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Germany to join our family in the east, that is to say, in Poland.
But we settled in a border town, Katowice.
This is when my brother and I started learning Polish.
And we were later enrolled in a Jewish high school-- it was known as gymnasium-- that was prestigious.
By the name of-- it carried the name of Firstenburg in Bendzin.
This was a Jewish gymnasium of high-quality education that included Jewish and Hebrew education, a kind of preparation for the Zionist dream of eventually going to what was then Palestine.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It is there where I learned Hebrew.
People often ask me whether I lived in Israel.
But I spoke Hebrew before there was an Israel.
I actually, much to my regret, never did live in Israel.
Still, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] our goal was to go to Palestine.
But in those days, the admission of Jews to Palestine was limited to capitalists, people with financial resources.
I don't remember exactly what the requirements were, but I know that we could not meet them.
Merchant.
Fairly well is the correct description.
We were not rich, but we were not poor.
And we lived on a fair standard.
Oh, yes.
That school cost money, but to my parents, no price was too high for the children's education.
If there was money to be saved, it would have been saved on anything else but on the children's education.
Exactly.
Before the war, that is to say, in end of August of 1939, we could see the dark clouds gathering.
And we escaped deeper into Poland, since we lived on the German border, to Tarnow, where my father had a brother.
We wound up in Tarnow on the day that war broke out.
And I remember exactly the-- the air assault on the city with the Stuka bombers right overhead.
And we-- that is to say, the people of the complex-- running into the cellar for shelter.
Yes, I remember it.
My uncle was a shochet of Tarnow.
I don't know how to describe a shochet.
It is a slaughterer of--
Of a ritual nature.
He was a very prominent man in the town of Tarnow community of Jews.
And he was a most pleasant personality, always with a smile.
The kindest person you ever met was my uncle in Tarnow.
To him, it mattered nothing how many people are in his house.
He always had an open house.
He fed anybody who came along.
He did well-- fairly well for the kind of life that the Jews lived in eastern Poland.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We stayed with his family.
Same, it's Zwass.
Yisroel Zwass.
Yisroel Zwass is a very prominent name among-- was at the time-- amongst Jewish population in Tarnow, everybody knew him.
By the way, while we are on the subject, his family consisted of wife and five wonderful children, one married daughter who had already a child.
Out of this family, only one girl survived.
She-- she-- my father later found her in Poland, brought her to Munich, and we were raised together.
She was then married, and she had a family of two children, who live here.
Each of her children has four children in turn, and there isn't one of them more delightful than the other.
Nothing but precious, all of them.
She lost her husband later on cancer, and remarried, and lives now in-- in Toronto.
Very close to me, she has become much like a sister.
Her children, too.
Yes.
We stayed with my uncle.
I already said, he always smiled.
Nothing bothered him, and we were a family of four.
It wasn't that convenient for him, but it meant nothing to him, though.
A wonderful host.
I have no idea about Tarnow.
I only know that the Germans killed all of them.
Except for this one girl-- Ida is her name.
She survived and, strangely enough by coincidence, she just about was parallel with us in the camps that we sent-- we were sent to.
In Tarnow we were until towards the end of 1939, when we went to Gorlice, where my grandparents and my mother's siblings lived.
And we tried to reestablish ourselves.
But soon, the anti-Jewish laws came into effect.
We were limited in-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in mobility, and we were locked, kind of, in-- in certain neighborhoods that were meant for Jews only.
This was not quite a ghetto in the sense it wasn't closed, but it was something like it.
It was a precursor of it.
We were made to wear a Star of David, a yellow star.
And-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I would quote Wiesel's description of it, 'our world was made to be smaller and smaller.' It started just then.
